coredata.dc:title: Novel in-situ composites of chlorine-doped CQDs and g-C3N4 with improved interfacial connectivity and accelerated charge transfer to enhance photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jiaqi
Chen, Congjin
Tan, Cen
Fan, Hui
Lu, Jingping
Li, Yingjie
Hu, Huayu
coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic performance of graphite-phase carbon nitrides (g-C3N4) composites is severely limited by poor interfacial connectivity and a narrow visible light response range. Although carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can enhance the visible light absorption of g-C3N4, it is difficult to produce effective interfacial connectivity due to their different properties. Herein, a novel “in-situ composite modified by chlorine-doped CQDs” strategy was proposed to further enhance the photocatalytic redox capability of g-C3N4. Specifically, chlorine-doped CQDs (Cl-CQDs) were prepared from mulberry branch powder and sulfoxide chloride directly, and the novel metal-free photocatalyst (Cl-CCN) was prepared by in-situ compounding the Cl-CQDs with g-C3N4. Cl-CCN shows superior photocatalytic tetracycline degradation rate (0.0269 min−1) and hydrogen production rate (1397.5 µmol g−1h−1), which are 14.9 and 2.75 times higher than those of g-C3N4, respectively. The characterization and DFT calculation results reveal that the C − Cl covalent bonds serve as fast electronic channels to bridge the hydrophilic Cl-CQDs and hydrophobic g-C3N4 and significantly enhance the interfacial coupling and charge transfer. On this basis, a possible mechanism for tetracycline degradation and hydrogen production photocatalyzed by Cl-CCN is proposed. This study provides a concise and economical method for modifying and enhancing the photocatalytic activity of composites CQDs/g-C3N4.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic performance of graphite-phase carbon nitrides (g-C3N4) composites is severely limited by poor interfacial connectivity and a narrow visible light response range. Although carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can enhance the visible light absorption of g-C3N4, it is difficult to produce effective interfacial connectivity due to their different properties. Herein, a novel “in-situ composite modified by chlorine-doped CQDs” strategy was proposed to further enhance the photocatalytic redox capability of g-C3N4. Specifically, chlorine-doped CQDs (Cl-CQDs) were prepared from mulberry branch powder and sulfoxide chloride directly, and the novel metal-free photocatalyst (Cl-CCN) was prepared by in-situ compounding the Cl-CQDs with g-C3N4. Cl-CCN shows superior photocatalytic tetracycline degradation rate (0.0269 min−1) and hydrogen production rate (1397.5 µmol g−1h−1), which are 14.9 and 2.75 times higher than those of g-C3N4, respectively. The characterization and DFT calculation results reveal that the C − Cl covalent bonds serve as fast electronic channels to bridge the hydrophilic Cl-CQDs and hydrophobic g-C3N4 and significantly enhance the interfacial coupling and charge transfer. On this basis, a possible mechanism for tetracycline degradation and hydrogen production photocatalyzed by Cl-CCN is proposed. This study provides a concise and economical method for modifying and enhancing the photocatalytic activity of composites CQDs/g-C3N4.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the environmental pollution and energy crises becoming increasingly severe in recent years, renewable energy sources, such as solar and hydrogen energy, have become increasingly popular [1,2]. Photocatalytic technology is an attractive solution to this problem because it captures solar energy for wastewater treatment and hydrogen production with the advantages of high efficiency, environmental friendliness, and sustainability [3,4]. An effective photocatalyst is a prerequisite for improving photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, it is crucial to develop efficient and sustainable photocatalysts [5]. Owing to the mild reaction conditions, no secondary pollution, and low reaction requirements [6], many semiconductor materials have been proposed for various photocatalytic applications. For example, TiO2-based [7,8] and Bi-based [9] materials have proven to be effective for removing organic pollutants and photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. However, their high cost, weak response to visible light, and noble metal residues severely limit their practical application [10].

As a metal-free conjugated semiconductor, graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has become a crucial photocatalyst owing to its efficient absorption of visible light, appropriate band gap, excellent thermal stability, simple synthesis, and low preparation cost. [11–13]. However, limited specific surface area, rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, and insufficient visible-light responsivenessseverely restrictits applications [12]. Nowadays, several strategies have been proposed for improving the performance of g-C3N4, including elemental doping [14], heterostructure formation [15], defect induction [16], morphology modulation [17], and coupling with carbon nanomaterials [18]. Notably, coupling carbon nanomaterials with g-C3N4 can compensate for the shortcomings of g-C3N4 by increasing the utilization rate of visible light, improving charge separation efficiency, enhancing light durability [19], and accelerating electron leap from the nanocomposite by inducing a synergistic effect using the electron transfer ability of carbon nanomaterials [13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel zero-dimensional (0 D) carbon nanomaterial, stands out among carbon-based materials as a highly anticipated new device due to its unique nanoscale size, high water solubility, excellent optoelectronic properties, nontoxicity, and low cost [20,21]. Compared to conventional photocatalytic materials, CQDs have upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) characteristics [22], which can convert absorbed long wavelengths into short wavelengths, thus generating more carriers and improving its photocatalytic ability. In addition, CQDs possess differentially emitting traps, can transfer electrons, and can extend the lifetime of carriers [20,23]. These advantages have led to the widespread application of CQDs in photocatalysis, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices [22]. Based on these properties, many studies have compounded CQDs with g-C3N4 to improve its photocatalytic activity. For example, Mou et al. prepared CN/N, Fe-CQDs composite materials, and it showed an 81.0% degradation efficiency of tetracycline (TC) after 40 min of exposure to visible light, whichis 1.79 times greater than that of pure g-C3N4 [24]. Zhang et al. prepared metal-free CQDs-modified g-C3N4 photocatalysts (CCN) both improved the photodegradation efficiency (k = 0.214 min−1) of Rhodamine B and the hydrogen production rate (1291 μmol g−1h−1) [25].

Nevertheless, the expensive precursors of CQDs and the complicated preparation process, as well as the relatively loose interfacial coupling between CQDs and g-C3N4, limit the performance improvement of g-C3N4 [26,27]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and economical method for CQDs synthesis and to enhance the interfacial contact between CQDs and g-C3N4. Compared with pure and expensive chemical substances, preparing CQDs from biomass waste is an economical means to effectively promote waste utilization and increase the content of oxygen-containing functional groups, thus significantly improving their electrical conductivity and photoluminescence [28]. Sim et al. successfully prepared AD-CQDs by a hydrothermal method using Arabic dates as the carbon source and compounded it with g-C3N4 to prepare AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalytic material. It achieved the 100% degradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP) within 90 min [29]. Mulberry (Morus alba L) is one of the characteristic local agricultural plants, whose leaves have a high medicinal value and are widely used in sericulture. However, it is challenging to fully utilize mulberry branches as a residue. Therefore, this study used a local waste mulberry branch and crushed it into a powder as a CQDs precursor to achieve the high-value utilization of biomass resources and provide an innovative approach for utilizing other woody biomass wastes for resource recovery.

For CQDs/g-C3N4, it is difficult to establish a tight interfacial connection between the hydrophilic CQDs and hydrophobic g-C3N4, which affects the charge transfer rate and limits the photocatalytic process. This problem can be effectively solved using a doping strategy. Recently, it has been demonstrated that common nonmetallic elements, such as N, S, and B, are often used as dopants that are introduced into CQDs to modify them with surface functional groups, thus significantly promoting the electron transfer efficiency between g-C3N4 and CQDs [30,31]. For example, Wang et al. prepared S-CQDs by adding L-cysteine as a sulfur source and achieved 82.67% degradation of TC in a complex with hollow tube g-C3N4 [32]. In addition, Murali et al. demonstrated that the Cl significantly affects the structure and chemical characteristics of CQDs, thereby influencing the photocatalytic process [33]. Hu et al. demonstrated that introducing chlorine onto the surface of CQDs induces downward bandgap bending, which leads to chlorine-doped CQDs (Cl-CQDs) exhibiting higher photocatalytic activity [34]. However, the preparation of Cl-CQDs generally requires a complicated multistep substitution process, and the photocatalytic activity of Cl-CQDs alone is limited.

In this study, Cl-CQDs were directly prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using mulberry branch powder and sulfoxide chloride (SOCl2) as precursors, and an in-situ recombination strategy with Cl-CQDs and urea as feedstock to prepare a tight-contact Cl-CQDs/g-C3N4 composite (Cl-CCN). To the best of our knowledge, chlorine-modified mulberry branch-based CQDs composites with g-C3N4 have not yet been reported. The interaction between the Cl-CQDs with g-C3N4 requires further study. Herein, through functionalized modification of CQDs, Cl-CQDs were successfully prepared and grafted onto g-C3N4 by C − Cl covalent bonding through a simple in situ growth method. Thus, Cl-CCN photocatalyst with tight interfacial connection and high photocatalytic activity was prepared. The photocatalytic redox characteristics of Cl-CCN were investigated using the TC degradation and hydrogen production tests. In addition, the pervasiveness, stability, reaction mechanism of Cl-CCN, and possible TC degradation pathways were analyzed to further understand the photocatalytic reaction process. This study provides novel insights into the preparation and performance enhancement of CQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalysts.
Chemical reagent details are provided in Supplementary Material text S1.

A one-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare biomass-based CQDs. First, the mulberry branch powder (4.0 g) was suspended in 50 mL of deionized water, followed by gradually adding 2 mL of SOCl2 solution. After thorough mixing, the suspensions were transferred to a 100 mL Teflon reactor and heated to 200 °C for 12 h. Second, to remove excess SOCl2, the resulting brown solution was heated at 100 °C for 2 h after cooling to room temperature. Then, after centrifugation, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain a solution of Cl-CQDs, which was purified by dialysis using a 1000 Da dialysis bag. Finally, the Cl-CQDs powder was obtained by drying the solution in a freeze dryer. CQDs were obtained without SOCl2 as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CN, CCN and Cl-CCN
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process for the composites is illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, urea (6 g) and different masses of Cl-CQDs powder were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water with stirring and ultrasonicated for 3 h to dissolve thoroughly. Then, the mixed solution was dried in an oven, and the dried solids were fully ground. Finally, the mixed brown powder was heated to 550 °C in a tube oven at a temperature gradient of 5 °C min−1, which was maintained for 2 h. The resulting brown powder was labeled as Cl-CCN. CN was prepared in the absence of Cl-CQDs. CCN was obtained from CQDs and urea using the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and density functional theory calculation
ce:section.ce:para: Details of characterization for the samples are provided in Supplementary Material text S2 and the density functional theory (DFT) calculation method is provided in Supplementary Material text S3.

TC was used as the target pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation activity of the prepared catalysts. A 300 W xenon lamp with a UV filter (λ > 420 nm) was selected as the light source. The pH of initial solution was maintained at 7 ± 0.1, while the reaction temperature was maintained at 25 ± 1 °C. 40 mg of different catalysts were suspended in 40 mL of the TC solution (20 mg/L). Firstly, the suspensions were mixed for 30 min in the dark to ensure adsorption–desorption equilibrium before illumination. During the photocatalytic process, the suspension was withdrawn in 3 mL from the photocatalytic tube at 30 min intervals and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Next, the solutions were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (U-5100, Shanghai Yuanxi Instruments, China), and the TC concentration was measured at λ = 357 nm. To reduce errors, all reactions were performed three times and the average was determined. Equation (1) was used to calculate the removal efficiency of TC. The experimental data was fitted to a first-order reaction kinetics model and Eq. (2) was used to determine the apparent rate constant.

where t (min) is the reaction time, C 0 is the initial TC concentration, Ct is the TC concentration at t (min), and k (min−1) is the apparent rate constant.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution was used to evaluate the photocatalytic reduction ability of the different catalysts. A 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a UV filter (λ > 420 nm) served as the light source for the photocatalytic process. The initial pH of the solution and the reaction temperature was maintained at 7 ± 0.1 and 6 ± 1 °C, respectively, during the experiment. First, 50 mg of each catalyst was suspended with 90 mL of deionized water in a quartz reactor, and 10 mL of the sacrificial agent triethanolamine (TEOA) was added. Subsequently, 3% H2PtCl6 was dissolved in the solution and sonicated for 30 min to maintain homogeneity. The mixture was continually mixed at 600 rpm during the reaction to ensure homogeneity. Before the reaction began, the light was opened for 30 min to deposit the Pt nanoparticles. The hydrogen produced during the process was collected hourly using an automatic sampler (CEL-GSOA-20, Education Golden Light, China). The hydrogen volume was measured using gas chromatograph (Nexis GC-2030, Shimadzu). The Au-Optical Power software was used to measure the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE).

To detect the major active species in TC degradation catalyzed by Cl-CCN, 5 mM isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and EDTA-2Na were introduced into the suspension as the trapping agents to capture •OH radicals, O2 – radicals, and holes (h+), respectively. Moreover, electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to capture the free radical signals.

TEM and SEM were used to observe the nanometer sizes and microstructures of the CN and Cl-CCN catalysts, respectively. Fig. 2 a–c shows TEM images of Cl-CCN. The porous nanosheet structure of Cl-CCN is shown in Fig. 2a. This is because of the large amount of gas generated during the high temperature calcination of the precursor, which provided a pore-forming template to form a large number of mesoporous structures on the surface of g-C3N4. These mesopores not only improved the structure of the material and increased its specific surface area, but also provided more active sites for the photocatalytic process. Fig. 2b and 2c indicate that the Cl-CQDs (marked by yellow circles) were successfully and uniformly distributed on the surface of g-C3N4, which laterally confirms the tight interfacial connection between the Cl-CQDs and g-C3N4 and the increase in charge transfer efficiency. HRTEM revealed lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.22 nm, which correspond to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon. SEM images of CN are illustrated in Fig. 2d, which show a multilayer graphitized nanosheet structure with curled edges. Compared to CN, Cl-CCN exhibited a consistent microstructure in (Fig. 2e and 2f), which indicated that addition of Cl-CQDs did not affect the microstructure of CN. Fig. 2g and k showed the elemental mapping and EDS diagram of Cl-CCN, from which Cl-CCN contained four elements, C, N, O and Cl, that confirmed the successful compounding of Cl-CCN. According to the previous literature [34], SOCl2 easily produces HCl and SO2 when in contact with water, which leads to a low chlorine content in Cl-CQDs and low Cl levels in Cl-CCN. In addition, the elemental mapping and EDS analysis of CN are shown in Fig. S1. The comparison shows a weight percentage (w%) increase in the elemental carbon (from 55.27% to 58.85%) and oxygen contents (from 2.40% to 3.62%) of Cl-CCN after the introduction of Cl-CQDs. It is due to the numerous oxygen-containing functional groups in the Cl-CQDs. According to Fig. S2, the EDS results reveal that the Cl content in the Cl-CQDs was 3.84% (w%), confirming the successful introduction of Cl.

FT-IR and XRD analyses were performed to examine the sample compositions and crystal structures of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 3 a, Cl-CCN, CCN, and CN have similar FT-IR spectra, indicating that Cl-CQDs and CQDs do not change the structure of CN. The peaks of the CQDs are not observed in the FT-IR spectrum because of their low content. The broad peaks at 3000–3500 cm−1 are due to the presence of N − H/O − H bonds, while multiple peaks appear in the range of 1200–1650 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN heterocycle. The peak at 813 cm−1 is the breathing mode of the triazine unit [35]. The XRD spectrum is shown in Fig. 3b. CN exhibit two characteristic diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.4°, corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystallographic planes of g-C3N4, respectively [30]. It shows that the addition of Cl-CQDs and CQDs weakened the typical peaks of g-C3N4 and led to a slight blue shift because it reduced the crystallinity of the triazine units and their stacking in g-C3N4 [36]. According to the results of the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm tests (Fig. 3c–e), all photocatalysts showed type IV curves, and the hysteresis loop was type H3. The specific surface area of Cl-CCN (55.26 m2/g) and CCN (52.03 m2/g) is significantly larger than that of CN (44.17 m2/g), suggesting that the introduction of Cl-CQDs and CQDs can enhance the specific surface area of the catalyst, thus shortening the mass transfer path and providing more active sites. A possible explanation for this is that the introduction of the different CQDs increased the interlayer distance of g-C3N4, thereby increasing the specific surface area [24]. In addition, Cl-CCN had a slightly higher specific surface area compared to CCN. This demonstrates the effectiveness of Cl-CQDs combined with g-C3N4 to increase photocatalyst specific surface area. Moreover, the increased pore volume is also beneficial for the adsorption and catalytic processes.

The catalysts were analyzed by XPS to further understand the elemental composition and chemical state of the catalyst surface. The survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a shows that both CN and Cl-CCN contain three elements: carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The oxygen content of Cl-CCN (3.56%) is greater than that of CN (2.77%) with the addition of Cl-CQDs, which is consistent with the EDS results. In addition, a smaller peak in Cl-CCN appears at 200.1 eV with a local zoom placed in the upper left corner of Fig. 4a, representing the Cl 2p orbital. Fig. 4b shows the C 1s spectra of CN and Cl-CCN, which contain three significant peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.1 eV for both Cl-CCN and CN, representing C−C/C= C, C−NH2, and N−C=N bonds, respectively, where C−NH2 may be due to the incomplete polymerization at the g-C3N4 edge [37]. In the N 1s spectra (Fig. 4c), Cl-CCN are deconvoluted into product three peaks located at 398.6 eV, 400.1 eV, and 401.1 eV, representing pyridine nitrogen (C−N=C, N1), graphite nitrogen ((C)3−N, N2), and pyrrole nitrogen (N−H, N3), respectively [38]. As shown in Fig. 4f, the N1/(N2 + N3) ratio in Cl-CCN is 3.307, which is higher than that in CN (2.718), proving the presence of more hybridized nitrogen in Cl-CCN. This laterally demonstrates the formation of non-homogeneous junctions and enhances the interfacial interaction between Cl-CQDs and g-C3N4 [39]. Furthermore, an increase in N1 leads to a decrease in the local work function, which accelerates O2 adsorption and provide more Lewis-base sites, thus improving the photocatalytic activity [40]. As shown in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d), the deconvolution of CN represents O−H bonding at 532.4 eV, which is possibly caused by water adsorption on the surface of the CN [41]. In contrast, the O 1s spectrum of Cl-CCN demonstrates two peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.6 eV, proving the existence of both C−O and O−H bonds and laterally confirming the successful introduction of Cl-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4e, the spectrum exhibits large fluctuations owing to the low Cl content in Cl-CCN. However, it is still clearly divided into four different peaks. The peaks at 200.9 eV and 199.3 eV correspond to the Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 of the C−Cl covalent bond, respectively. While the two peaks at 198.2 eV and 197.2 eV represent the binding energies of Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 of the surface chloride ions, respectively [42]. This result confirms the presence of C−Cl covalent bonds in Cl-CCN. In contrast, the Cl 2p spectrum of Cl-CQDs is in Fig. 4e shows only two peaks, the Cl 2p1/2 at 197.8 eV and Cl 2p3/2 at 199.5 eV in chloride ion signal peaks [43]. Fig. S3 shows the detailed XPS results for the Cl-CQDs. These results indicated that Cl was successfully introduced into the CQDs to synthesize Cl-CQDs through an elemental doping strategy. During the complexation of Cl-CQDs with g-C3N4, the Cl atom in Cl-CQDs combined with the C atom in g-C3N4 to form a C−Cl covalent bond, indicating that Cl-CQDs were firmly anchored to g-C3N4. It was confirmed that the interfacial connection between hydrophilic Cl-CQDs and hydrophobic g-C3N4 was strengthened by the formation of C−Cl bonds.

Photoelectric properties are crucial for photocatalysts. Therefore, in this study, various catalysts were optically and electrochemically characterized. The UV–Vis DRS spectrum reflects the degree of light absorption by the photocatalyst (Fig. 5 a). The absorption band edge of CN is approximately 454 nm. While the absorption intensity of the composite catalysts increased significantly with the addition of different CQDs because they can serve as photosensitizers and optical centers [44], especially Cl-CCN (483 nm). It suggests that Cl-CQDs can significantly enhance the light absorption properties of CN compared with CQDs. To further explore the catalyst band structure, the band gaps of the different catalysts were measured using the Kubelk-Munk method. As shown in the inset in Fig. 5a, the bandgap of Cl-CCN is 2.64 eV, significantly smaller than those of CCN (2.73 eV) and CN (2.75 eV). It indicates that Cl-CCN can absorb and utilize more visible light and generate more carriers, thus improving photocatalytic activity. This result suggests that Cl-doping can effectively change the band gap of the CQDs [44], thus affecting the composite catalyst.

Fig. S4a illustrates the flat-band potential (EFB) results of the semiconductor photocatalysts using the Mott–Schottky analysis. The EFB of CN, CCN, and Cl-CCN is –0.81 eV, –0.76 eV, and –0.68 eV, respectively. It indicates that the addition of different CQDs reduced the EFB of the catalysts, especially for Cl-CQDs. It means that in Cl-CCN, the electrons located at the Fermi energy level in g-C3N4 are more easily transferred to the Cl-CQDs than in CCN and CN. This can facilitate effective charge transfer and separation and promote the photocatalytic activity [45]. The reason might be that new electronic states can be generated in the energy band structure by chlorine ions or organic molecules. It is due to the interaction between the unsaturated bonds terminating on the Cl-CQD surfaces [46,47]. In addition, the slopes of different samples are positive, indicating features of n-type semiconductors. Compared with EFB, ECB (the conduction band potential) is usually negative approximately 0.2 V for n-type semiconductors [48]. Therefore, the calculated ECB of CN, CCN, and Cl-CCN are –1.01 eV, –0.96 eV, and –0.88 eV, respectively. The bandgap of the Cl-CCN photocatalyst is narrower and exhibits stronger light absorption, as shown in the catalyst energy band diagram (Fig. 5b).

To further evaluating the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers, CN, CCN, and Cl-CCN were subjected to PL analyses. In general, lower fluorescence intensities indicate a lower electron–hole recombination efficiency and higher photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 5c, the PL emission spectrum of CN under 380 nm excitation showed an intense emission peak at 480 nm, whereas the carbon quantum dot modified CCN and Cl-CCN exhibited a lower emission peak. It indicates that both the CQDs and Cl-CQDs complexed with CN can significantly suppress the recombination of photogenerated carriers in the composites, particularly in the case of Cl-CQDs. It is due to the tight connection formed between Cl-CQDs and g-C3N4 by the C−Cl bonds, which effectively accelerate the electron transfer rate and inhibit carrier recombination. Moreover, in the PL spectra, both CCN and Cl-CCN exhibited a “blue shift” caused by the upconversion effect of the CQDs [41].

Fig. 5d illustrates the transient photocurrent responses of the different photocatalysts under intermittent visible light illumination. The higher photocurrent density in Cl-CCN indicates a more efficient photogenerated charge transfer rate between the Cl-CQDs and g-C3N4. The instability of the first cycle might have been caused by the light source just irradiating to the reaction system. While the catalyst response current remained almost unchanged over the next four cycles, indicating its stability. In addition, catalyst conductivity was studied using EIS and the results are presented in Fig. 5e. In the EIS Nyquist plot, the smaller the semicircle radius, the smaller the corresponding charge resistance, the higher the charge transfer efficiency, and the better the conductivity [49]. Obviously, Cl-CCN has the smallest half-circle radius, which proves that Cl-CQDs can effectively reduce g-C3N4 resistance. Additionally, it indicates that the C−Cl bonds formed during complexation could accelerate charge transfer at the composite interface. The CV curves in Fig. 5f show that Cl-CCN has the largest peak potential difference and reversible circulation area compared to those of CN and CCN. This result indicates that Cl-CCN has a higher current density and stronger charge storage capacity [50]. It further confirms that Cl-CCN can perform charge transfer and separation more efficiently and improve the redox capacity of the material, leading to a higher photocatalytic activity.

The electronic recombination structures of the catalysts were analyzed using the total density of states (TDOS) and projected density of states (PDOS). As shown in Fig. 6 a, the TDOS of Cl-CCN around the Fermi energy level is significantly larger than those of Cl-CQDs and g-C3N4, implying that Cl-CCN has a higher conductivity and carrier mobility [51]. This result agrees with the PL, EIS, and I-t analyses. Combined with the PDOS (Fig. 6b), the orbitals of C 2p and Cl 2p showed synchronous peaks near the Fermi energy level. It indicates that stable C−Cl covalent bonds can be formed by orbital hybridization [52], which enhances the interfacial linkage between the hydrophobic g-C3N4 and hydrophilic Cl-CQDs. Also, it indicated laterally that the formation of C−Cl bonds can affect electron flow and thus act as electron channels to increase electron transfer rate [53]. Based on the above characterization and calculation results, it was confirmed that the Cl-CQDs were successfully anchored to the surface of the g-C3N4 nanosheets by an in situ composite strategy using stable C − Cl bonds, resulting in an effective and tight interfacial connection. Furthermore, electrons can flow more rapidly to Cl-CQDs with the help of C−Cl bonds, indicating that interfacial chemical bonds can act as electron bridges to provide electron channels at the interface [54], thus enhancing the interfacial electron transfer of the composites and effectively improving the photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic degradation capacities of the different catalysts were determined using TC degradation using irradiation with visible light as the index. Among them, the adsorption of TC by the different materials under dark conditions is shown in Fig. S4b. The adsorption of TC by Cl-CCN is better than that of CN and CCN, which agrees with the results of the BET tests. The larger specific surface area of Cl-CCN provides more adsorption sites. It also indicates that a tight interfacial connection is conducive to surface adsorption. All the photocatalysts reached adsorption equilibrium within 30 min in the dark. Therefore, the adsorption experiments were performed for 30 min before the light was opened for subsequent measurement experiments. TC degradation resulting from photocatalysis using different catalysts are shown in Fig. 7 a. The efficiency of TC self-degradation is very low without catalysts and it can be considered that it is difficult to degrade under visible light. Additionally, pure Cl-CQDs were significantly more efficient than CQDs in the TC photocatalytic degradation (Fig. S4c). It is owing to the coexistence of oxygen and chlorine on the Cl-CQDs, which form upward and downward energy band bends, respectively, creating an internal miniature electric field and accelerating electron–hole separation [34]. The degradation efficiency was significantly enhanced by the other catalysts, especially the Cl-CCN-x catalyst, where x represents the mass of the added Cl-CQDs. The TC degradation efficiency by Cl-CCN-5 reached 91.7% after illumination for 120 min, which was 3.89 and 2.07 times higher than that of CN (23.6%) and CCN (44.3%), respectively. The catalytic results for the different catalysts within 1 h were simulated using a quasi-first-order kinetic model. Fig. 7b shows that all the catalysts followed quasi-first-order reaction kinetics. After fitting the curves, the maximum apparent kinetic rate constant k for Cl-CCN was 0.0269 min−1, which was 14.9 and 6.40 times higher than that of CN (0.0018 min−1) and CCN (0.0042 min−1), respectively. Furthermore, the highest photocatalytic activity of Cl-CCN was obtained with the addition of 5 mg of Cl-CQDs. This is because an appropriate amount of Cl-CQDs serves as an electron transfer medium to facilitate the separation of photoelectrons and holes. In contrast, excess Cl-CQDs would compete with g-C3N4 for photons, resulting in an internal filtration effect [32]. Therefore, Cl-CCN-5 was chosen as the best catalyst for all subsequent tests and denoted as Cl-CCN. The degradation rates of the catalysts prepared in this study were compared with those of previously reported CQDs/g-C3N4-based catalysts, and the results presented in Table S1 demonstrate the superiority of Cl-CCN.

Natural water systems are generally complex and can easily interfere with degradation. In this study, pH values and common anions were selected for investigation, and their effects on TC degradation were examined separately. The TC degradation efficiencies of Cl-CCN under different pH conditions are shown in Fig. 7c. It gradually increased with increasing pH and reached a maximum when pH was 9. To further illustrate the influence of the pH, the zero-point charge was determined by titration. The pHi value corresponding to the zero point charge in this system was determined to be 5.20, and the detailed results are shown in Fig. S4d. Many inorganic anions in natural water affect degradation process. Therefore, the effect of common anions in water bodies on the photocatalytic process was determined in this study, as shown in Fig. 7d. The degradation process was slightly promoted due to the TC solution becoming alkaline after the addition of HCO3 − and HPO4 − [55]. While Cl− and SO4 2− had inhibitory effects on the photocatalytic degradation owing to the scavenging of free radicals and competitive adsorption with TC molecules [56]. Due to its two-sided effect, NO3 − had little impact on this process. On the one hand, it inhibits light absorption. On the other hand, it can generate other free radicals in the presence of illumination, thereby promoting degradation reactions [57]. In general, Cl-CCN could be stable in complex water systems. Cl-CCN was used to separately degrade different organic pollutants to probe the suitability of the material (Fig. 7e). At an initial concentration of 10 mg/L, after 30 min of dark adsorption and 120 min of illumination, the photocatalytic degradation efficiencies by Cl-CCN were 52.0%, 63.5%, 96.7%, and 99.6% for Ofloxacin (OFX), Ciprofloxacin (CIP), Rhodamine B (RhB) and Methyl Orange (MO), respectively. This result confirmed the excellent applicability of Cl-CCN. The TC mineralization ratio was evaluated by measuring the total organic carbon (TOC) by the different catalysts (Fig. S4e). After 30 min of darkness and 120 min of irradiation, the TOC of TC photocatalytic degradation by Cl-CCN reached 18.6%, which is higher than those of CN (7%) and CCN (10.2%). Five cycles were performed to determine the recyclability (Fig. 7f). After five cycles, the degradation of TC by Cl-CCN decreased by 10.4% and the effective degradation of TC was maintained.

The photocatalytic reduction capacities of the different catalysts were measured using the evolution rate of hydrogen irradiated with visible light as an index. Hydrogen production of the CQDs, Cl-CDQs, and other comparative experiments are shown in Fig. S4f. Hydrogen was not produced in the dark because photogenerative electrons could not be produced. Only a small amount of hydrogen was produced because the electrons quickly recombined with holes due to the anaerobic environment without sacrificial agent. Due to the catalytic effect of Pt, some hydrogen can be produced even without catalyst. The hydrogen yield of the Cl-CQDs was higher than that of the CQDs, indicating the excellent photocatalytic activity of the Cl-CQDs. Fig. 8 a–c illustrate the effects of different catalysts on producing hydrogen. As shown in Fig. 8a, CN, CCN, and Cl-CCN exhibit stable photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiencies within 4 h, which is related to the excellent chemical stability of g-C3N4. Combining Fig. 8a and 8c, CN shows the minimum hydrogen release rate (507.1 μmol g−1h−1). With the introduction of CQDs, the hydrogen release rate increased, especially for Cl-CCN (1397.5 μmol g−1h−1), which was significantly higher than that of CCN (813.6 μmol g−1h−1). It demonstrates that Cl-CCN can increase the hydrogen release rate because it can more effectively accelerate the charge transfer within the interface than CN and CCN. Fig. 8c shows the AQE of the different samples, where the AQE of Cl-CCN (1.44%) is 2.88 times higher than that of CN (0.50%). According to Fig. 8b, with the increasing Cl-CQDs content, the hydrogen production performance of the Cl-CCN-x photocatalysts first increased and then decreased. Among them, Cl-CCN-5 exhibited the best photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance, which is consistent with the degradation experiments. This is because excessive carbon dots would compete with g-C3N4 for photons, which reduces the light utilization of g-C3N4 and leads to a decrease in the hydrogen evolution efficiency [58]. In addition, the Cl-CCN photocatalyst could maintain similar photocatalytic hydrogen production activity after 20 h of irradiation, indicating that the photocatalyst has good photostability (Fig. 8d). Table S2 shows a comparison with other reported CQDs/g-C3N4-based catalysts, demonstrating the excellent photocatalytic reduction ability of Cl-CCN. FT-IR and XRD measurements of the used samples were performed (Fig. 8e and 8f). Comparing to fresh samples, the structural composition and crystalline phase of Cl-CCN do not change significantly after use. These results indicate that the prepared Cl-CCN catalysts maintains its activity and stability during repetition.

UPLC-MS was performed to determine the intermediates produced during TC photocatalytic degradation by Cl-CCN. The mass spectra of the major intermediates (Fig. S5) reflected three possible pathways in the degradation process, as shown in Fig. 9 . In Pathway I, the TC molecules were first attacked by the active species, and intermediates T1 (m/z = 417) and T2 (m/z = 401) were obtained by demethylation and deamidation. Then the amide and hydroxyl groups were removed to produce the products T3 (m/z = 359) and T4 (m/z = 343). Finally, T5 (m/z = 261), T6 (m/z = 121), and T7 (m/z = 136), which are small organic molecules, were obtained through continuous ring-opening reactions. However, in Pathway II, TC first formed T8 (m/z = 462) by hydroxylation and to produce T9 (m/z = 419) by demethylation in the presence of light and Cl-CCN, followed by obtaining product T10 (m/z = 365) through further demethylation and deamidation. Finally, the products T11 (m/z = 194) and T12 (m/z = 176) were generated by ring opening and dehydroxylation reactions, respectively. Pathway III is a catalytic process in which the conjugated double bond on TC is first opened to form T13 (m/z = 481), followed by generation of T14 (m/z = 453) and T15 (m/z = 437) through the demethylation and deamidation reactions. Subsequently, T15 undergoes dehydroxylation and oxidation to generate small organic molecules, including T16 (m/z = 393), T17 (m/z = 274), and T18 (m/z = 163). Eventually, these intermediates were oxidatively degraded into smaller molecular materials, which were further mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

Radical scavenging tests and ESR analyses were performed to better understand the contributions of the major active species during the photocatalytic process. It is shown in Fig. 10 a and 10b. The radical scavenging experiments revealed a specific decrease in the photocatalytic activity when different radical trapping agents were added to the reaction system, and the greater the degree of decline, the more critical the associated free radicals. As shown in Fig. 10a, the TC degradation efficiency decreased dramatically in the presence of p-BQ and EDTA-2Na, 32.9% and 58.6%, respectively. Compared with EDTA-2Na, p-BQ showed a greater inhibitory effect. It illustrates that O2 – radicals and h+ are the major active species during this reaction, and the role of O2 – radical is greater than that of h+. In contrast, the response was not significantly inhibited when IPA was present, indicating that •OH radicals did not significantly affect the photocatalysis process. It is consistent with the energy band test results shown in Fig. 10b. Since the CB of Cl-CCN is –0.88 eV, which is more negative than the standard potential of O2/O2 – (–0.33 eV vs. NHE) and H+/H2 (0 eV vs. NHE) [25]. In contrast, the VB of Cl-CCN is + 1.76 eV, which is smaller than the standard potential of •OH/OH– (+2.4 eV vs. NHE) [25], making it difficult to produce •OH. Fig. 10b shows the ESR results under the DMPO spin trap. In the dark state, no characteristic signal corresponding to O2 – was detected. However, the appearance of the DMPO–O2 – characteristic peak after 10 min of visible light indicated that O2 – was produced only under illumination, confirming the crucial role of O2 – in the photocatalytic process.

Considering the above results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for Cl-CCN is proposed (Fig. 10c). In the degradation process, TC molecules were first adsorbed on the surface of Cl-CCN by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic gravitational forces. Next, when visible light excites Cl-CCN, the electrons (e–) in the VB of g-C3N4 were excited and transferred to the CB. Because the Cl-CQDs and g-C3N4 were tightly bonded through C − Cl bonds, electrons were further transferred to the Cl-CQDs, thus achieving the effect of inhibiting the recombination of e– and h+ pairs and strengthening the photocatalytic activity. The continuously accumulated e– reacted with O2 in the environment to produce O2 – and promoted pollutant degradation. Moreover, with the continuous transfer of e–, a large amount of h+ remained in the VB of g-C3N4, which further contributed to pollutant decomposition.

However, in the hydrogen production process, Cl-CCN generated a large number of electron-hole pairs under visible light excitation. The excess h+ was consumed by the sacrificial agent TEAO, which effectively prevented the complexation of e– and h+ and assisted in the catalytic reduction process. And the continuous accumulation of e– on the surface of Cl-CCN provided a strong reducing ability, which benefited hydrogen production. When Pt was added as a co-catalyst, some of the e– could be further transferred to adjacent Pt nanoparticles, leading to more efficient water reduction and H2 generation. Moreover, the Cl-CQDs were also used as photosensitizers for photocatalysis. It could strengthen the visible-light absorption region of the catalyst so that it absorbed more photons and generated more photogenerated carriers. Consequently, Cl-CCN exhibited a better photocatalytic activity than CN and CCN.

In this study, a novel photocatalyst (Cl-CCN) was successfully constructed using an in-situ composite method of Cl-doped CQDs prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method and g-C3N4. The apparent rate constants of TC degradation by Cl-CCN were 14.94 and 6.40 times higher than those of CN and non-chlorine-doped CCN, respectively, and the hydrogen production rates were 2.75 and 1.72 times greater than those of CN and CCN, respectively. Thus, the addition of Cl-CQDs enhanced the photocatalytic redox capacity of g-C3N4. The efficient photocatalytic capacity of Cl-CCN was attributed to the formation of tight interfacial connections between the Cl-CQDs and g-C3N4 through the C−Cl covalent bonds, which serve as a fast electron channel, accelerate charge transfer efficiency and improve photocatalytic activity. In addition, the recyclability and universality of Cl-CCN in photocatalytic reactions were also confirmed and possible TC degradation pathways were inferred using UPLC-MS. The results of free radical trapping experiments and ESR were combined to demonstrate the dominant role of O2 – and h+, thus possible photocatalytic mechanisms were proposed. These results provide new insights for designing more efficient CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysts.
